respective parent corporations, and such foreign airlines is disclosed in certain reports and statements filed 
with the SEC.  Such reports and statements can be inspected and copied at the public reference facilities 
maintained by the SEC, which can be located by calling the SEC at 1 800 SEC 0330.  The SEC maintains 
a website containing reports, proxy statements and other information regarding registrants that file 
electronically with the SEC.  In addition, each airline is required to file periodic reports of financial aid and 
operating statistics with the U.S. DOT.  Such reports can be inspected at the U.S. DOT’s Office of Airline 
Information, Bureau of Transportation Statistics, Department of Transportation, 1200 New Jersey Avenue, 
S.E., Washington, D.C. 20590, and copies of such reports can be obtained from U.S. DOT at prescribed 
rates. 
Foreign airlines serving LAX, or foreign corporations operating airlines serving LAX (unless such 
foreign airlines have ADRs registered on a national exchange), are not required to file information with the 144 
SEC.  Such foreign airlines, or foreign corporations operating airlines serving LAX, file limited information 
only with the U.S. DOT. 
See “CERTAIN INVESTMENT CONSIDERATIONS – Demand for Air Travel; Aviation Activity 
and Related Matters,” “– Financial Condition of the Airlines; Effect of Airline Industry Consolidation; 
Effect of Airline and Concessionaire Bankruptcies” and “—Aviation Safety; Security Concerns; Cyber 
Security.” 
The Department undertakes no responsibility for and makes no representation as to the 
accuracy or completeness of (i) any reports and statements filed with the SEC or U.S. DOT as 
described in this section or (ii) any material contained on the SEC’s website as described in this 
section, including, but not limited to, updated information on the SEC website or links to other 
Internet sites accessed through the SEC’s website.  Any such information is not part of this Official 
Statement nor has such information been incorporated by reference herein, and such information 
should not be relied upon in deciding whether to invest in the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds. 
LITIGATION REGARDING THE AIRPORT SYSTEM AND THE DEPARTMENT 
General 
From time to time, the Department is a party to litigation and is subject to claims arising out of its 
normal course of business and operations.  At this time, there is no pending litigation relating to the Airport 
System or the Department’s operations or business pertaining thereto that would reasonably be expected to 
have a material impact on Net Pledged Revenues or the operation of LAX. 
Certain Construction Litigation and Other Construction Disputes 
Construction of the West Gates at the Tom Bradley International Terminal, formerly referred to as 
the Midfield Satellite Concourse – North Project, was completed and became operational in Fiscal Year 
2021.  The project cost was approximately $1.7 billion.  Turner-PCL, a Joint Venture (“TPJV”), contracted 
with the Department to design and build the project.  TPJV has filed suit against the Department for over 
$220 million in claims for additional compensation.  Their claims are broad ranging and cover COVID-19 
claims for direct costs and delays for extended overhead; additional general conditions and general 
requirements for over-inspection and additional Department non-contractual requirements; additional costs 
related to code compliance disputes; and additional costs related to unforeseen conditions and design issues.  
The Department has rejected these claims and believes that they have little or no merit.  The Department 
has filed its own claim against TPJV for liquidated damages for approximately $14 million.  The 
Department and TPJV are currently engaged in mediation.  If the parties cannot reach a settlement through 
mediation, the case will proceed with discovery and litigation of the claims.  The Department cannot predict 
the ultimate outcome of this litigation. 
Additionally, as described under “AIRPORT CAPITAL PLANNING – Major Existing Capital 
Program Projects – Landside Access Modernization Program”, the APM Developer has asserted various 
relief event claims under the APM Agreement, and the ConRAC Developer has asserted various relief event 
claims under the ConRAC Agreement. 
LITIGATION REGARDING THE SERIES 2022GHI SENIOR BONDS 
There is no litigation now pending or, to the best of the Department’s knowledge, threatened which 
seeks to restrain or enjoin the sale, execution, issuance or delivery of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds or 
in any way contests the validity of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds or any proceedings of the Board taken 145 
with respect to the authorization, sale or issuance of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds, or the pledge or 
application of any moneys provided for the payment of or security for the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds. 
TAX MATTERS 
General 
In the opinion of Kutak Rock LLP, Bond Counsel to the Department, under existing laws, 
regulations, rulings and judicial decisions, interest on the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds is excluded from 
gross income for federal income tax purposes, except for interest on any Series 2022G Senior Bond or 
Series 2022H Senior Bond for any period during which such Series 2022G Senior Bond or Series 2022H 
Senior Bond is held by a “substantial user” of the facilities financed or refinanced by the Series 2022G 
Senior Bonds or the Series 2022H Senior Bonds, as applicable, or a “related person” within the meaning of 
Section 147(a) of the Code.  Bond Counsel is further of the opinion that (a) interest on the Series 2022G 
Senior Bonds and the Series 2022H Senior Bonds constitutes an item of tax preference for purposes of the 
federal alternative minimum tax, and (b) interest on the Series 2022I Senior Bonds is not a specific 
preference item for purposes of the federal alternative minimum tax imposed on individuals.  The opinions 
described above assume the accuracy of certain representations and compliance by the Department with 
covenants designed to satisfy the requirements of the Code that must be met subsequent to the issuance of 
the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  Failure to comply with such requirements could cause interest on the 
Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds to be included in gross income for federal income tax purposes retroactive 
to the date of issuance of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  The Department has covenanted to comply 
with such requirements.  Bond Counsel has expressed no opinion regarding other federal tax consequences 
arising with respect to the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds. 
The accrual or receipt of interest on the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds may otherwise affect the 
federal income tax liability of the owners of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  The extent of these other 
tax consequences will depend on such owners’ particular tax status and other items of income or deduction.  
Bond Counsel has expressed no opinion regarding any such consequences.  Purchasers of the Series 
2022GHI Senior Bonds, particularly purchasers that are corporations (including S corporations and foreign 
corporations operating branches in the United States of America), property or casualty insurance 
companies, banks, thrifts or other financial institutions, certain recipients of social security or railroad 
retirement benefits, taxpayers entitled to claim the earned income credit, taxpayers entitled to claim the 
refundable credit in Section 36B of the Code for coverage under a qualified health plan or taxpayers who 
may be deemed to have incurred or continued indebtedness to purchase or carry tax-exempt obligations, 
should consult their tax advisors as to the tax consequences of purchasing or owning the Series 2022GHI 
Senior Bonds. 
Bond Counsel is further of the opinion that interest on the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds is exempt 
from present State of California personal income taxes. 
A copy of the proposed form of opinion of Bond Counsel is attached hereto as Appendix E.  
Tax Treatment of Original Issue Premium 
The Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds that have an original yield below their respective interest rates, 
as shown on the inside cover of this Official Statement (collectively, the “Premium Series 2022GHI Senior 
Bonds”), are being sold at a premium.  An amount equal to the excess of the issue price of a Premium Series 
2022GHI Senior Bond over its stated redemption price at maturity constitutes premium on such Premium 
Series 2022GHI Senior Bond.  A purchaser of a Premium Series 2022GHI Senior Bond must amortize any 
premium over such Premium Series 2022GHI Senior Bond’s term using constant yield principles, based on 146 
the purchaser’s yield to maturity (or, in the case of Premium Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds callable prior 
to their maturity, generally by amortizing the premium to the call date, based on the purchaser’s yield to 
the call date and giving effect to any call premium).  As premium is amortized, the amount of the 
amortization offsets a corresponding amount of interest for the period, and the purchaser’s basis in such 
Premium Series 2022GHI Senior Bond is reduced by a corresponding amount resulting in an increase in 
the gain (or decrease in the loss) to be recognized for federal income tax purposes upon a sale or disposition 
of such Premium Series 2022GHI Senior Bond prior to its maturity.  Even though the purchaser’s basis 
may be reduced, no federal income tax deduction is allowed.  Purchasers of the Premium Series 2022GHI 
Senior Bonds should consult with their tax advisors with respect to the determination and treatment of 
premium for federal income tax purposes and with respect to the state and local tax consequences of owning 
a Premium Series 2022GHI Senior Bond. 
Tax Treatment of Original Issue Discount 
General.  The Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds that have an original yield above the respective 
interest rate as shown on the inside cover of this Official Statement (collectively, the “Discount Series 
2022GHI Senior Bonds”), are being sold at an original issue discount.  The difference between the initial 
public offering prices of such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds and their stated amounts to be paid 
at maturity constitutes original issue discount treated in the same manner for federal income tax purposes 
as interest, as described above. 
The amount of original issue discount that is treated as having accrued with respect to such Discount 
Series 2022GHI Senior Bond or is otherwise required to be recognized in gross income is added to the cost 
basis of the owner of the bond in determining, for federal income tax purposes, gain or loss upon disposition 
of such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond (including its sale, redemption or payment at maturity).  
Amounts received on disposition of such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond that are attributable to 
accrued or otherwise recognized original issue discount will be treated as tax-exempt interest, rather than 
as taxable gain, for federal income tax purposes. 
Original issue discount is treated as compounding semiannually, at a rate determined by reference 
to the yield to maturity of each individual Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond, on days which are 
determined by reference to the maturity date of such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond.  The amount 
treated as original issue discount on such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond for a particular semiannual 
accrual period is equal to the product of (i) the yield to maturity for such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior 
Bond (determined by compounding at the close of each accrual period) and (ii) the amount that would have 
been the tax basis of such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond at the beginning of the particular accrual 
period if held by the original purchaser, (b) less the amount of any interest payable for such Discount Series 
2022GHI Senior Bond during the accrual period.  The tax basis for purposes of the preceding sentence is 
determined by adding to the initial public offering price on such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond the 
sum of the amounts that have been treated as original issue discount for such purposes during all prior 
periods.  If such Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond is sold between semiannual compounding dates, 
original issue discount that would have been accrued for that semiannual compounding period for federal 
income tax purposes is to be apportioned in equal amounts among the days in such compounding period. 
Owners of Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds should consult their tax advisors with respect 
to the determination and treatment of original issue discount accrued as of any date, with respect to when 
such original issue discount must be recognized as an item of gross income and with respect to the state and 
local tax consequences of owning a Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bond.  Subsequent purchasers of 
Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds that purchase such bonds for a price that is higher or lower than 
the “adjusted issue price” of the bonds at the time of purchase should consult their tax advisors as to the 
effect on the accrual of original issue discount. 147 
Recognition of Income Generally.  Section 451 of the Code was amended by Pub. L. No. 115-97, 
enacted December 22, 2017 (sometimes referred to as the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act) to provide that taxpayers 
using an accrual method of accounting for federal income tax purposes generally will be required to include 
certain amounts in income, including original issue discount, no later than the time such amounts are 
reflected on certain financial statements of such taxpayer.  The application of this rule may require the 
accrual of income earlier than would have been the case prior to the amendment of Section 451 of the Code.  
Investors should consult their own tax advisors regarding the application of this rule and its impact on the 
timing of the recognition of income related to the Discount Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds under the Code. 
Backup Withholding 
As a result of the enactment of the Tax Increase Prevention and Reconciliation Act of 2005, interest 
on tax-exempt obligations such as the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds is subject to information reporting in 
a manner similar to interest paid on taxable obligations.  Backup withholding may be imposed on payments 
fails to provide certain required information including an accurate taxpayer identification number to any 
person required to collect such information pursuant to Section 6049 of the Code.  The reporting 
requirement does not in and of itself affect or alter the excludability of interest on the Series 2022GHI 
Senior Bonds from gross income for federal income tax purposes or any other federal tax consequence of 
purchasing, holding or selling tax-exempt obligations. 
Changes in Federal and State Law 
From time to time, there are legislative proposals in the Congress and in the various state 
legislatures that, if enacted, could alter or amend the federal and state tax matters referred to above or 
adversely affect the market value of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  It cannot be predicted whether or 
in what form any such proposal might be enacted or whether if enacted it would apply to bonds issued prior 
to enactment.  In addition, regulatory actions are from time to time announced or proposed and litigation is 
threatened or commenced which, if implemented or concluded in a particular manner, could adversely 
affect the market value of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  It cannot be predicted whether any such 
regulatory action will be implemented, how any particular litigation or judicial action will be resolved, or 
whether the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds or the market value thereof would be impacted thereby.  
Purchasers of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds should consult their tax advisors regarding any pending or 
proposed legislation, regulatory initiatives or litigation.  The opinions expressed by Bond Counsel are based 
upon existing legislation and regulations as interpreted by relevant judicial and regulatory authorities as of 
the date of issuance and delivery of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds, and Bond Counsel has expressed no 
opinion as of any date subsequent thereto or with respect to any pending legislation, regulatory initiatives 
or litigation. 
RATINGS 
Moody’s Investor Service Inc. (“Moody’s”), S&P Global Ratings, a division of Standard & Poor’s 
Financial Services LLC (“S&P”) and Fitch Ratings (“Fitch”), have assigned ratings of “Aa2,” “AA” and 
“AA,” respectively, to the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  Such ratings reflect only the views of such 
organizations and any desired explanation of the significance of such ratings should be obtained from the 
rating agency furnishing the same, at the following addresses: Moody’s, 7 World Trade Center, 250 
Greenwich Street, New York, New York 10007; S&P Global Ratings, 55 Water Street, 38 th Floor, New 
York, New York 10041; and Fitch, 33 Whitehall Street, New York, New York 10004.  The Department 
furnished the rating agencies with certain information and materials concerning the Series 2022GHI Senior 
Bonds and the Department, some of which is not included in this Official Statement.  Generally, a rating 
agency bases its rating on the information and materials furnished to it and on investigations, studies and 
assumptions of its own.  There is no assurance that the ratings assigned to the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds 148 
will continue for any given period of time or that such ratings will not be revised downward or withdrawn 
entirely by one or more of the rating agencies, if in the judgment of such rating agencies, circumstances so 
warrant.  Any such downward revision or withdrawal of such ratings may have an adverse effect on the 
market prices of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.

UNDERWRITING 
The Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds are being purchased from the Department by Goldman Sachs 
& Co. LLC, on its own behalf and on behalf of Academy Securities, Inc., Samuel A. Ramirez & Co., Inc., 
and SMBC Nikko Securities America, Inc., the underwriters of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds 
(collectively, the “Underwriters”).  The Underwriters will purchase the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds at a 
price of $1,308,017,061.63 (consisting of the aggregate principal amount of the Series 2022GHI Senior 
Bonds of $1,183,380,000.00, plus a net original issue premium of $127,176,306.25, less an underwriters’ 
discount of $2,539,244.62), subject to the terms of the Bond Purchase Agreement (the “Bond Purchase 
Agreement”), between Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, as representative of the Underwriters, and the 
Department. 
The Bond Purchase Agreement provides that the Underwriters shall purchase all of the Series 
2022GHI Senior Bonds if any are purchased, and that the obligation to make such purchase is subject to 
certain terms and conditions set forth in the Bond Purchase Agreement, the approval of certain legal matters 
by counsel, and certain other conditions.  The Underwriters may change the initial public offering prices 
set forth on the inside front cover page of this Official Statement.  The Underwriters may offer and sell the 
Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds to certain dealers (including dealers depositing the applicable Series 
2022GHI Senior Bonds into investment trusts) at prices lower than the public offering prices or at yields 
higher than the yields stated on the inside front cover page of this Official Statement. 
The following two paragraphs have been provided by the Underwriters for inclusion in this Official 
Statement and the Department does not make any representation as to their accuracy or completeness. 
Certain of the Underwriters and their respective affiliates are full service financial institutions 
engaged in various activities, which for certain of the Underwriters may include securities trading, 
commercial and investment banking, advisory, investment management, principal investment, hedging, 
financing and brokerage activities.  Certain of the Underwriters and their respective affiliates have, from 
time to time, performed, and may in the future perform, various investment banking services for the 
Department, for which they received or will receive customary fees and expenses. 
In the ordinary course of their various business activities, the Underwriters and their respective 
affiliates may make or hold a broad array of investments and actively trade debt and equity securities (or 
related derivative securities) and financial instruments (which may include bank loans and/or credit default 
swaps) for their own account and for the accounts of their customers and may at any time hold long and 
short positions in such securities and instruments.  Such investment and securities activities may involve 
securities and instruments of the Department.  The market activities of the Underwriters and other market 
participants may impact the value of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  The Underwriters have indicated 
that their respective affiliates may also communicate independent investment recommendations, market 
color or trading ideas and/or publish or express independent research views in respect of such assets, 
securities or instruments and may at any time hold, or recommend to clients that they should acquire, long 
and/or short positions in such assets, securities and instruments. 150

SCOPE OF THIS 2022GHI LETTER REPORT 
This 2022GHI Letter Report summarizes our update of certain assumptions in (1) the “Report of 
the Airport Consultant on the proposed issuance of Los Angeles International Airport 
Subordinate Revenue Bonds, 2021 Series D and Subordinate Refunding Revenue Bonds 2021 
Series E” (collectively, the Series 2021DE Bonds), dated September 14, 2021 (the 2021DE 
Report), and (2) the “Letter Report of the Airport Consultant on the proposed issuance of Los 
Angeles International Airport Subordinate Revenue and Subordinate Refunding Revenue Bonds, 
2022 Series A through 2022 Series F” (collectively, the Series 2022A-F Bonds), dated December 
23, 2021 (the 2022A-F Letter Report), which were used to forecast, among other things, the 
average airline cost per enplaned passenger (CPE) and debt service coverage from Fiscal Year
1 
(FY) 2022 through FY 2027, referred to in the Prior Reports (as defined below) as the Forecast 
Period.  The Forecast Period presented on the financial exhibits attached to this 2022GHI Letter 
Report has been extended through FY 2030 to include completion of the existing Airport Capital 
Program (the Existing Airport Capital Program) and the Next Airport Capital Program, as 
discussed in the sections below.  
The 2021DE Report and the 2022A-F Letter Report are referred to collectively as the Prior 
Reports in this 2022GHI Letter Report.  This 2022GHI Letter Report and the Prior Reports should 
be read in their entirety for an understanding of the forecasts and the underlying assumptions 
discussed herein.   
Based on our review, it was determined that, for purposes of this 2022GH Letter Report, the 
key assumptions and forecasts described in the Prior Reports are appropriate for the purposes 
of this 2022GH Letter Report, except for the changes in the key assumptions summarized 
below.  See the “UPDATES TO KEY ASSUMPTIONS” section below for more detailed information. 
• Enplaned Passenger Forecast:  Based on more recent actual numbers of enplaned 
passengers and scheduled seats data, the forecast of enplaned passengers was updated.  
As reflected on Figure 2 later in this 2022GH Letter Report, the updated numbers of 
forecast enplaned passengers for FY 2022 through FY 2024 is higher than in the Prior 
Reports, while the updated numbers of forecast enplaned passengers for FY 2025 
through FY 2027 has not changed materially.  The extended Forecast Period that includes 
FY 2028 through FY 2030 assumes the same increase in enplaned passengers of 1.7% per 
year as FY 2025 through FY 2027, which is the average annual 20-year (FY 1999-FY 2019) 
rate of growth in the number of enplaned passengers at the Airport.  
• Federal Grants:  This 2022GHI Letter Report incorporates approximately $1.1 billion 
more in federal grants in the financial forecasts compared to the amounts in the Prior 
Reports, mostly because certain federal grants had not yet been awarded to the 
Department or the Department had not finalized plans for the use of such grants when 
 
1 The City’s Fiscal Year ends June 30. Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-3 
the Prior Reports were prepared.  The additional grants reduce certain annual capital 
and LAX Maintenance and Operation (M&O) Expenses. 
• Airport Capital Program:  The Existing Airport Capital Program through FY 2026 has 
increased 1.7% from $11.5 billion reflected in the Prior Reports to $11.7 billion in this 
2022GHI Letter Report (as reflected in Exhibit A).  Certain funding sources have changed 
as further described later in this 2022GHI Letter Report.   
As of the date of this 2022GHI Letter Report, Department management was identifying 
and prioritizing future Airport capital improvements that would be part of the Next 
Airport Capital Program.  In the Prior Reports, certain projects that were still in the 
preliminary planning stages were referred to as “Other Projects” and were not included 
in the Airport Capital Program.  These Other Projects are now included in the Next 
Airport Capital Program.  The Department currently expects to finalize the Next Airport 
Capital Program by the third quarter of 2022.  As described later in this 2022GH Letter 
Report, certain cost allowances for various types of potential projects (terminal, airfield, 
roadways, etc.) totaling approximately $15.0 billion to be constructed and available for 
use between  
FY 2023 and FY 2029 were assumed for the Next Airport Capital Program in the financial 
forecasts presented herein.   
• Debt Service:  This 2022GHI Letter Report incorporates actual rather than estimated 
debt service on the Series 2021DE Bonds and the Series 2022A-F Bonds, and the 
elimination of debt service associated with the (1) refunding of Series 2016A 
Subordinate Revenue Bonds, Series 2016C Senior Refunding Revenue Bonds, Series 
2012A Senior Revenue Bonds, and Series 2012B Senior Revenue Bonds and (2) 
restructuring of certain other debt service.  The Prior Reports did not incorporate any 
reduced debt service from these bond refundings.  In addition, the interest rate assumed 
for the issuance of Future Bonds has increased from 6.0% in the Prior Reports to 7.0% in 
this 2022GH Letter Report.  
• LAX M&O Expenses:  Forecasts of LAX M&O Expenses in this 2022GHI Letter Report are 
higher than those forecast in the Prior Reports because the updated forecasts are based 
on 6.0% base expense growth per year (rather than 5.0% per year), and a revised 
estimate of additional LAX M&O Expenses associated with future facilities under the 
Existing Airport Capital Program and the potential projects in the Next Airport Capital 
Program, the latter of which were not known as of the date of the Prior Reports.  The 
updated forecasts of LAX M&O Expenses are based on the Department’s FY 2023 
Budget, whereas the forecasts of LAX M&O Expenses in the Prior Reports were based on 
the Department’s FY 2022 Budget.  Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-4 
• Nonairline Revenues:  Forecasts of nonairline revenues2 in this 2022GHI Letter Report 
are higher than those forecast in the Prior Reports because they are based on the FY 
2023 Budget of nonairline revenues prepared by the Department (rather than the FY 
2022 Budget) and assumed increases associated with future facilities for both the 
Existing Airport Capital Program and the Next Airport Capital Program, the latter of 
which were not known as of the date of the Prior Reports.   
UPDATES TO KEY ASSUMPTIONS 
Enplaned Passenger Forecast 
The worldwide outbreak of novel coronavirus SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) began in late 2019 and 
caused significant disruptions to domestic and international passenger travel as well as the 
conduct of day-to-day business in the City of Los Angeles, the rest of the United States and the 
world.  The numbers of flights and passengers on the passenger airlines serving the Airport 
have been and continue to be lower than they were in 2019 because of the COVID-19 
pandemic.   
Recovery in the number of enplaned passengers at the Airport is related to increases in 
domestic passenger travel, which has been consistent with national recovery trends as shown 
on Figure 1, but recovery in the total number of enplaned passengers (domestic and 
international) has been slower at the Airport than in the nation as a whole as a result of the 
large share of international passenger traffic typically accommodated at the Airport.  
Restrictions imposed by governments around the world, including, but not limited to, 
mandatory quarantine periods, proof of a negative COVID-19 test, or bans on non-essential 
travel have more severely curtailed international travel than domestic travel.   
For the 12 months ended November 30, 2021 (the latest available Bureau of Transportation 
Statistics data), the Airport was the third busiest international gateway in the nation (it was the 
second busiest U.S. international gateway in 2019 and the third busiest in 2020), with 
international enplaned passengers accounting for 15.8% of total enplaned passengers at the 
Airport compared with an average share of 9.4% of international enplaned passengers at other 
large-hub U.S. airports.  The consistent ranking of the Airport from 2020 through the 12-month 
period ended November 30, 2021, was largely attributable to the different international 
markets served from the Airport compared with those served from other international gateway 
airports and the loosening or elimination of travel restrictions or border closures at those 
destinations.  
 
2 Nonairline revenues include Aviation Revenues and Concession Revenues shown in Exhibit C of this 2022GH 
Letter Report.  Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022

3, which decreased a total of 65.9% in 2020 and

 A-6 
At the Airport, the numbers of enplaned passengers decreased 28.9% in FY 2020 and 67.0% in 
FY 2021 compared with the number enplaned in FY 2019.  As shown on Figure 1, the recent 
growth in the numbers of enplaned passengers at the Airport, which is largely the result of 
growth in domestic traffic, has been consistent with that at other international gateway 
airports in the nation.  
The numbers of enplaned passengers at the Airport during the first 10 months of FY 2022 (July 
2021 through April 2022) continued to recover to approach the number enplaned in 2019, as 
follows:  
• The number of domestic enplaned passengers increased to 77.1% of the number of 
domestic enplaned passengers in 2019. 
• The number of international enplaned passengers increased to 42.4% of the number of 
international enplaned passengers in 2019. 
• The number of total enplaned passengers increased to 66.9% of the number of total 
enplaned passengers in 2019. 
The number of enplaned passengers during the first 10 months of FY 2022 equaled 
approximately 66.9% of the number enplaned in FY 2019, a substantially higher recover as 
compared to 27.8% during the same period in FY 2021.  
The forecasts of enplaned passengers at the Airport in this 2022GHI Letter Report were based 
on the following major assumptions, which are expected to result in faster recovery in the 
number of enplaned passengers at the Airport from FY 2022 through FY 2025 compared with 
the forecasts presented in the 2021DE Report, as shown on Figure 2: 
• Forward-looking scheduled flight and seat data published by the airlines serving the 
Airport for May 2022 and June 2022 (the last 2 months of FY 2022) and from July 2022 
through October 2022 (the first 4 months of FY 2023). 
• Assumed passenger boarding load factors for the months described immediately above 
equal to load factors recently experienced at the Airport.  
• Thereafter, an assumed monthly recovery in the number of domestic and international 
enplaned passengers at the Airport.   
While the forecasts of enplaned passengers do not include any specific assumptions regarding 
potential reductions in scheduled flight activity in June 2022 (the last month of FY 2022) and 
the first and second quarters of FY 2023 resulting from, but not limited to, pilot and other flight 
crew shortages and recent increases in aircraft fuel costs, we believe that the forecasts of 
enplaned passengers are sufficiently conservative to account for these and other factors.  In 
addition, and as discussed later in this 2022GH Letter Report, economic activity in the Los Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-7 
Angeles Combined Statistical Area4 (CSA)—a key driver of passenger traffic at the Airport—has 
remained relatively stable (population), increased (nonagricultural employment), or is 
recovering (unemployment rate) to pre-COVID-19 pandemic levels, which will further increase 
the demand for airline travel.  See Table 1 of this 2022GHI Letter Report for additional 
information.  
On February 7, 2022, Frontier Airlines and Spirit Airlines announced plans to merge the 
operations of both airlines.  On April 8, 2022, JetBlue Airways announced plans to purchase 
Spirit Airlines and on May 16, 2022, JetBlue Airways announced plans for a takeover of Spirit 
Airlines.  Based on the number of enplaned passengers at the Airport in FY 2021, the combined 
airline would have accounted for approximately 7.7% (Frontier/Spirit) or 11.0% (JetBlue/Spirit) 
of total Airport enplaned passengers, making the combined operations of both airlines the sixth 
(Frontier/Spirit) or fourth (JetBlue/Spirit) busiest at the Airport as measured by numbers of 
enplaned passengers.   
In the forecast of enplaned passengers presented in this 2022GH Letter Report, it was assumed 
that there would be no material change in the operations of the combined airlines at the 
Airport given that a significant number of enplaned passengers on Frontier, JetBlue, and Spirit 
airlines are originating (100.0% on Frontier, 97.7% on JetBlue, and 95.7% on Spirit based on  
FY 2021 data) and are not connecting passengers.  
The numbers of domestic and international enplaned passengers at the Airport are forecast to 
reach and exceed FY 2019 numbers in FY 2025, and to increase 1.7% per year thereafter, which 
is the average annual 20-year (FY 1999-FY 2019) rate of growth in the number of enplaned 
passengers at the Airport.  Specifically, it was forecast that: 
• Domestic enplaned passenger numbers at the Airport would reach FY 2019 numbers by 
FY 2024.  
• International enplaned passenger numbers at the Airport would reach FY 2019 numbers 
by FY 2025.  
 
4 The Los Angeles CSA includes Los Angeles, Orange, Riverside, San Bernardino, and Ventura counties. Ms. Beatrice Hsu

Economic Information 
The economy of an airport service region is a major factor affecting long-term airline traffic at 
the airport(s) serving the region.  In general, regions with large populations, an extensive 
employment base, and increasing levels of per capita personal income will generate demand 
for airline travel.  The demographics and economy of the region—as measured by changes in 
population, nonagricultural employment, and per capita personal income—as well as airline 
service and airfares are typically the most important factors affecting origin and destination 
(O&D) passenger demand at airport(s) serving the region.   
As shown in Table 1, the population of the Los Angeles CSA decreased from 18.6 million in 2020 
to 18.5 million in 2021, while the unemployment rate decreased 28.8% (from 11.1% in 2020 to 
7.9% in 2021) and nonagricultural employment increased 6.8% (from 10.7 million in 2020 to 
11.5 million in 2021) as employers rehired employees laid off during the COVID-19 pandemic.  
Even at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the unemployment rate was 11.1%, 
lower than the unemployment rate of 12.4% experienced in the Los Angeles CSA during the 
2008/2009 financial crisis.

 A-11 
Airport Capital Program 
Department management periodically develops and updates a Capital Program for the 
redevelopment, improvement, and expansion of Airport facilities (Airport Capital Program).  
The Airport Capital Program is developed based on anticipated facility needs, current and 
forecast airline traffic, available funding sources, project priorities, and other relevant 
information that is available to the Department when the Airport Capital Program is developed.  
Existing Airport Capital Program.  The Existing Airport Capital Program, which is 
estimated to cost approximately $11.7 billion (see Exhibit A for costs and funding sources), was 
initiated in or around FY 2016 and is currently expected to be completed by the end of 
FY 2026.
5  According to the Department, approximately $7.4 billion of the Existing Airport 
Capital Program was completed through the end of FY 2022 and projects costing approximately 
$4.3 billion are ongoing or expected to be initiated and completed by the end of FY 2026.  The 
estimated cost of the Existing Airport Capital Program increased approximately $205 million, or 
1.7%, from the $11.5 billion estimated cost reflected in the Prior Reports.    
With completion of the Existing Airport Capital Program estimated to be in FY 2026, 
Department management will have substantially modernized and transformed the Airport into 
a world class facility focused on enhancing the passenger experience as a result of the following 
major improvements: 
• A new automated people mover system (APM System) that is to quickly transport 
passengers between a new consolidated rental car facility (ConRAC); intermodal 
transportation facilities that include, but are not limited to, new Airport public parking 
areas, a new ground transportation center for taxicabs and other commercial vehicles; 
and the Central Terminal Area (the CTA).  
• A new ConRAC easily accessed by passengers from any terminal in the Central Terminal 
Area via the APM System.  
• A renovated and enhanced Terminal 1, which is used by Southwest Airlines. 
• The renovation and integration of Terminals 2 and 3, and relocation of Delta Air Lines 
and SkyTeam partner airlines to those terminals. 
• A new midfield satellite concourse (MSC) with 12 new gates. 
• Renovation of and enhanced customer experience at Terminals 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8.  
 
5 As of the date of this 2022GHI Letter Report, the Department’s published Airport Capital Program totaled $15.1 
billion, which includes certain projects already completed, certain APM System and ConRAC Developer payments, 
and certain Terminal 4 Project expenditures outside the scheduled completion of the Existing Airport Capital 
Program.  See Table 3 in this 2022GHI Letter Report.   Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-12 
As shown in Exhibit A, the Department currently expects that the $11.7 billion Existing Airport 
Capital Program would be funded from (1) $3.9 billion of Department funds, (2) $5.6 billion of 
proceeds from the sale of prior bonds, (3) the net proceeds of approximately $974.4 million 
from the sale of the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds, and (4) $1.2 billion of other funds, 
including, but not limited to, passenger facility charge (PFC) revenues on a pay-as-you-go basis 
and federal grants-in-aid.  The Department currently does not expect to issue any Future Bonds 
(subsequent to the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds) for completion of the Existing 
Airport Capital Program.  The estimated funding sources shown in Exhibit A have not changed 
significantly from estimated funding sources reflected in the Prior Reports—total funding from 
prior and proposed Bonds decreased approximately $48.8 million, Department funds increased 
approximately $261.5 million, and other funds decreased approximately $9.0 million. 
Currently, the Department’s published capital program for LAX totals $15.1 billion.  This $15.1 
billion includes certain projects that have already been completed, certain APM System and 
ConRAC Developer payments, and a portion of the Terminal 4 Project cost that is outside the 
scheduled completion of the Existing Airport Capital Program (after FY 2026).  Table 3 
summarizes the differences between the published capital program of $15.1 billion and the 
$11.7 billion existing Airport Capital Program included in this 2022GH Letter Report.

Next Airport Capital Program. With the substantial completion of the Existing Airport 
Capital Program and the modernization and transformation of the Airport during the Forecast 
Period, Department management is now focused on identifying and prioritizing future Airport 
capital improvements that would be part of the Next Airport Capital Program.  The Department 
currently expects to finalize the Next Airport Capital Program after the date of this 2022GHI Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-13 
Letter Report in 2022.  Once finalized, the Next Airport Capital Program would begin prior to 
the completion of the Existing Airport Capital Program.  
As part of this process, Department management is evaluating differing levels of future capital 
investments and potential funding sources, and the effect, if any, future investments may have 
on key Airport financial metrics such as, but not limited to, average airline cost per enplaned 
passenger, debt service coverage, and discretionary Airport cash.   
After receiving certain environmental approvals, Department management recently initiated 
planning an Airfield & Terminal Modernization Project (the ATMP), which includes, but is not 
limited to, airfield improvements to increase operational efficiency, construction of a new 
concourse (Concourse Zero or Terminal 1 East) and up to 11 narrowbody gates
6 that would be 
connected to existing Terminal 1, construction of a new terminal (Terminal 9) and up to 12 
widebody gates7 on the southeast side of the Airport that would be connected to existing CTA 
roadways, and various roadway improvements.   
The projects included in the ATMP and other potential Airport projects8 identified by 
Department management, including, but not limited to, construction of an expansion of the 
Midfield Satellite Concourse (MSC) to the south, may be included in the Next Airport Capital 
Program, but there is no assurance that they will be included in the Next Airport Capital 
Program.  The completion of the Terminal 4 Project (project costs remaining after FY 2026) is 
also included in the Next Airport Capital Program. 
Because the Department currently expects to adopt the Next Airport Capital Program in 2022 
after the date of this 2022GHI Letter Report and to implement all or a portion of the Next 
Capital Program during the Forecast Period, certain allowances for future capital investments 
and funding sources associated with the Next Airport Capital Program have been assumed in 
the forecast of financial results presented in this 2022GH Letter Report, as follows:  
• Approximately $2.0 billion of capital investments per year from FY 2023 through FY 2029 
for a total of approximately $15.0 billion.   
• Because individual projects in the Next Airport Capital Program have not been identified 
by Airport cost center (e.g., Airfield, Terminals), it was assumed that the $15.0 billion in 
total assumed investments would be in the following cost centers: roughly 70.0% in 
Terminals, roughly 10.0% in Airfield and Apron Areas, and roughly 20.0% in Access and 
nonairline cost centers.  These assumed percentages are based on prior levels of 
investment by Airport cost center. 
 
6 While the proposed Concourse Zero would add up to 11 new gates, the Concourse Zero project would result in 
the loss of 2 Terminal 1 gates for a net addition of up to 9 new gates at the Airport.  
7 The proposed Terminal 9 project would include up to 12 widebody gates or 18 narrowbody gates. 
8 According to the Department, any redevelopment of existing cargo facilities at the Airport is currently expected 
to be funded by private developers from sources other than the issuance of Future Bonds.  Ms. Beatrice Hsu

• Approximately $8.8 billion in net proceeds from the issuance of Future Senior Bonds, 
approximately $2.6 billion in net proceeds from the issuance of Future Subordinate 
Bonds, and approximately $2.8 billion of Department funds were assumed to be used to 
fund Next Airport Capital Program project costs during the Forecast Period.  Allowances 
were included for financing and other costs associated with issuing Future Bonds.  
Future Bonds to be issued for Airfield and Apron improvements were assumed to be 
Future Subordinate Bonds, and all other Future Bonds were assumed to be Future 
Senior Bonds.   
• The Department has applied for federal grants and will continue to apply for future 
federal grants for projects to be included in the Next Airport Capital Program.  As 
reflected above on Table 2, the Department currently expects to use approximately 
$359.3 million of BIL grants to fund Next Airport Capital Program project costs through 
FY 2027 ($79.3 million awarded in FY 2022 plus an additional $280.0 million assumed 
through FY 2027).  The financial forecasts also assume the Department will receive 
approximately $500.0 million of AIP grants between FY 2023 and FY 2029 (in addition to 
the federal grants shown on Exhibit A) to help pay for projects in the Next Airport Capital 
Program. 
Prior to the date when the Next Airport Capital Program is finalized, the Department expects 
that the cost to implement the Next Airport Capital Program will be identified, better 
information becomes available related to construction costs, inflation, project scope, project 
phasing, and assumed method of project delivery. 
Exhibit A-1 presents a summary of the allowances and estimated sources of funds for the Next 
Airport Capital Program. 
Assumptions Included in Financial Forecasts. The financial forecasts presented in this 
2022GHI Letter Report include the following: 
• Assumed changes in airline and nonairline Pledged Revenues, LAX M&O Expenses, and 
Debt Service associated with financing, construction, and completion of the Existing 
Airport Capital Program.   
• Because specific projects to be included in the Next Airport Capital Program by 
Department management are not known as of the date of this 2022GH Letter Report, 
certain changes in Pledged Revenues, M&O Expenses, and Debt Service were assumed 
in the financial forecasts: 
o $10.6 billion in assumed capital investments in the Terminals for potential 
projects such as, but not limited to, Concourse 0, Terminal 9, and Terminal 4 
Project costs remaining after FY 2026.  The change in airline revenues associated 
with these investments was based, in part, on the rate-making methodology Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-15 
included in the Further Restated and Amended Rate Agreement and Tariff/Rate 
Methodology.   
o $1.3 billion in assumed capital investments in the Airfield and Apron Areas for 
potential projects such as, but not limited to, apron areas for Concourse 0 and 
Terminal 9.  The change in airline revenues associated with these investments 
was based on the rate-making methodology in the Airfield Certificate and 
Operating Permit (ACOP).   
o $2.5 billion in assumed capital investments in the Access Cost Center for 
potential projects such as, but not limited to, Airport roadway improvements and 
potential APM System stations.  Access Cost Center project costs are allocated 
between airline and nonairline cost centers pursuant to the Tariff.  
o $640.0 million in assumed other capital investments including infrastructure, 
utility, and other renewal and replacement improvements.  The change in 
nonairline revenues
9 assumes that the Department would increase nonairline 
revenues by an amount equal to approximately 75.0% of the sum of Senior Bond 
Debt Service, Subordinate Obligations Debt Service, and LAX M&O Expenses 
associated with nonairline cost center projects assumed to be included in the 
Next Airport Capital Program.  Between FY 2019 and FY 2021, nonairline 
revenues accounted for approximately 154.0% of the sum of Senior Bond Debt 
Service, Subordinate Obligations Debt Service, and LAX M&O Expenses in 
nonairline cost centers.

LAX Maintenance and Operation Expenses  
Forecasts of LAX M&O Expenses reflected in Exhibit D of this 2022GH Letter Report, which are 
calculated on the basis of LAX M&O Expenses in the FY 2023 Budget and an assumed 6.0% per 
year expense growth plus estimated LAX M&O Expenses associated with future facilities (for 
both the Existing Airport Capital Program and the Next Airport Capital Program), are slightly 
lower than forecasts of LAX M&O Expenses in the Prior Reports for FY 2023 and FY 2024, and 
higher starting in FY 2025.   
Forecasts of LAX M&O Expenses in the Prior Reports were based on the LAX M&O Expenses in 
the FY 2022 Budget and an assumed 5.0% per year expense growth plus estimated LAX M&O 
Expenses associated with future facilities in the Existing Airport Capital Program only.   
As reflected in Exhibit D, FY 2027 LAX M&O Expenses are forecast to be $1.2 billion in this 
2022GH Letter Report, approximately $28.7 million or 2.5% higher than FY 2027
10 LAX M&O 
Expenses forecast in the Prior Reports. 
LAX M&O Expenses are forecast to increase from approximately $917.6 million in FY 2023 
(before the use of Coronavirus Relief Grants) to approximately $1.5 billion in FY 2030, at an 
average annual growth rate of 7.8%. 
Nonairline Revenues  
Forecasts of nonairline revenues presented in Exhibit C of this 2022GHI Letter Report are based 
on the FY 2023 Budget of nonairline revenues and certain assumed increases associated with 
future facilities for both the Existing Airport Capital Program and the Next Airport Capital 
Program.  The updated forecasts of nonairline revenues are higher than forecasts of nonairline 
revenues in the Prior Reports.  Forecasts of nonairline revenues in the Prior Reports were based 
nonairline revenues in the FY 2022 Budget, and certain assumed increases associated with 
future facilities in the Existing Airport Capital Program only.

As reflected in Exhibit C, FY 2027 nonairline revenues are forecast to be $962.8 million11 in this

nonairline revenues forecast in the Prior Reports. 
Nonairline revenues are forecast to increase from approximately $603.3 million in FY 2023 to 
approximately $1.1 billion in FY 2030, at an average annual growth rate of 9.3%. 
PROPOSED SERIES 2022GHI SENIOR BONDS  
As presented in Exhibit B, the Department currently expects to issue the proposed Series 
2022GHI Senior Bonds to:  
• Finance a portion of the Existing Airport Capital Program.  
• Refund approximately $91.1 million in Subordinate Commercial Paper Notes used to pay 
the interest portion of debt service on certain Existing Airport Capital Program projects.  
• Fund capitalized interest on the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds. 
• Make a deposit to the Senior Debt Service Reserve Fund. 
• Pay issuance and financing costs associated with the issuance of the proposed Series 
2022GHI Senior Bonds.  
The proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds are assumed to be issued as fixed-rate bonds with 
the following final maturity dates and all-in true interest costs, based on input from Frasca & 
Associates, LLC (the Department’s Municipal Adviser):

As reflected in Exhibit A, the projects expected to be funded with the net proceeds from the

Terminals 
• North Terminal Improvement Program – Delta Air Lines.  This project includes the 
complete renovation of Terminal 3, renovations to Terminal 2, construction of a secure 
connector to the north side of Tom Bradley International Terminal (TBIT), infrastructure 
improvements supporting the planned APM System, and various enabling projects.  
Delta Air Lines is providing construction funding and undertaking these improvements, 
which are to be purchased by the Department in phases when the portions of the 
project are complete and have been included in the annual calculation of the Terminal 
Buildings Rate.  This project is estimated to cost $1.8 billion; approximately $387.4 
million of this cost is expected to be funded with net proceeds from the sale of the 
proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  This project is expected to be completed by the 
end of FY 2024. 
• Terminal 4 Improvement Project – American Airlines.  This project includes the 
construction of a connector between Terminal 4 and Terminal 5 that would connect to 
the new APM System.  This project is estimated to cost approximately $1.1 billion; 
approximately $180.6 million of this cost is expected to be funded with net proceeds 
from the sale of the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  This project is expected to 
be completed by the end of FY 2026.  American Airlines is providing construction funding 
and undertaking these improvements, which are to be acquired by the Department 
when the project is completed and has been included in the forecast Terminal Buildings 
Rate. 
The Department currently expects that an additional $665.0 million in improvements to 
Terminal 4 may be required to complete the renovation of that terminal, but that the 
$665.0 million of additional improvements would be completed after completion of the 
Existing Airport Capital Program by the end of FY 2026.  For purposes of this 2022GH 
Letter Report, the additional $665.0 million of additional costs for Terminal 4 are 
included in the costs of the Next Airport Capital Program discussed above (and reflected 
in Exhibit A-1).  The additional Pledged Revenues, LAX M&O Expenses, annual debt 
service payments, and other costs associated with the $665.0 million of future Terminal 
4 project costs are included in the financial forecasts in this 2022GH Letter Report. 
• Terminal 6 Project – Alaska Airlines.  This project includes the modernization of 
Terminal 6 by increasing the amount of holdroom space, enhancing the security 
screening checkpoint, installing new passenger boarding bridges, and implementing 
certain other operational improvements.  Under a letter of intent with the Department, 
Alaska Airlines would provide construction funding and undertake these improvements, 
which would be purchased by the Department in phases when portions of the project 
are complete and have been included in the annual calculation of the Terminal Buildings 
Rate.  This project is estimated to cost approximately $233.5 million; approximately Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-19 
$134.5 million of this cost is expected to be funded with net proceeds from the sale of 
the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  This project is expected to be completed by 
the end of FY 2024.  
• TBIT Core and APM System Interface.  This project is to provide vertical circulation 
elements in TBIT to accommodate passenger circulation and connections to the APM 
System.  While associated with the APM System, the cost of this project is to be 
allocated to the Airport’s Terminal cost center.  This project is estimated to cost $275.9 
million; approximately $30.4 million of this cost is expected to be funded with net 
proceeds from the sale of the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  This project is 
expected to be completed by the end of FY 2024. 
• Terminal 5 Core and APM System Interface.  This project will provide vertical circulation 
elements in Terminal 5 to accommodate passenger circulation and connections to the 
APM System.  While associated with the APM System, the cost of this project will be 
allocated to the Airport’s Terminal cost center.  This project is estimated to cost $215.1 
million; approximately $23.7 million of this cost is expected to be funded with net 
proceeds from the sale of the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  This project is 
expected to be completed by the end of FY 2024. 
Landside Access Modernization Program 
• APM System Construction.  This project includes milestone payments to the developer 
selected by the Department to design, build, finance, operate, and maintain a new APM 
System.  The APM System includes an elevated guideway structure, six APM stations 
(three within the Central Terminal Area and three outside the Central Terminal Area), 
APM trains, and an APM train maintenance facility.  The Department’s portion of this 
project is estimated to cost $1.7 billion; approximately $168.3 million of this cost is 
expected to be funded with the proceeds from the sale of the proposed Series 2022GHI 
Senior Bonds.  The elevated guideway structure to be used by APM System trains was 
completed in April 2022.  This project is expected to be completed by FY 2024.  
Remaining Projects 
• Power Distribution Facility:  This project replaces existing facilities and provides more 
reliable power transmission and greater capacity to support planned Airport growth.  
This project is estimated to cost approximately $159.2 million; approximately $49.5 
million of this cost is expected to be funded with net proceeds from the sale of the 
proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.  This project is expected to be completed by the 
end of FY 2024.

FUTURE SENIOR BONDS AND SUBORDINATE OBLIGATIONS   
As reflected in Exhibit A, following the issuance of the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds, 
the Department currently does not expect that any Future Bonds would be required for 
completion of the Existing Airport Capital Program.  As reflected in Exhibit A-1, approximately 
$8.8 billion of Future Senior Bond proceeds and $2.6 billion of Future Subordinate Bond 
proceeds are estimated to be needed to fund the Next Airport Capital Program. 
According to the Department’s Municipal Advisor, estimated annual debt service on Future 
Bonds was based on the following assumptions: 
• An interest rate of 7.00% on future Senior Bonds and future Subordinate Obligations. 
• Capitalized interest, including the refinancing of Commercial Paper Notes issued to fund 
capitalized interest, reserve funds, and other costs of issuance to be funded with the net 
proceeds from the sale of Future Bonds; debt service on Future Bonds included in the 
financial forecasts presented in this 2022GHI Letter Report is net of capitalized interest. 
• The Future Bonds issued for each project to be amortized over the project’s expected 
useful life or 30 years, whichever occurs first. 
Exhibit B presents estimated sources and uses for Future Bonds. 
As stated earlier, debt service savings from any outstanding Bonds that the Department may 
refund for economic savings during the Forecast Period are not included in the forecasts 
presented in this 2022GH Letter Report.

DEBT SERVICE COVERAGE PURSUANT TO THE REVENUE BOND INDENTURES 
Under the Senior Revenue Bond Indenture, Senior Bonds are secured by a pledge of Net 
Pledged Revenues and certain funds and accounts held by the Senior Trustee.  As defined in the 
Senior Revenue Bond Indenture, Net Pledged Revenues equal Pledged Revenues less LAX M&O 
Expenses. Pursuant to the Senior Revenue Bond Indenture, the Department has covenanted to 
establish, fix, prescribe, and collect rates, tolls, fees, rentals, and charges for the use of the 
Airport so that in each Fiscal Year: 
• Pledged Revenues are at least equal to the amount of required deposits to various funds 
and accounts during such Fiscal Year, and 
• Net Pledged Revenues, together with any Transfer (as defined in the Senior Revenue 
Bond Indenture), are equal to at least 125% of the Senior Bond Aggregate Annual Debt 
Service on outstanding Senior Bonds. 
In this 2022GH Letter Report, these covenants are referred to as the Senior Bond Rate 
Covenant. 
Although the Revenue Bond Indentures provide for a Transfer of certain amounts that can be 
included in the calculation of debt service coverage, no such Transfer was assumed in the 
financial forecasts in this 2022GH Letter Report. 
As shown on Figure 3, debt service coverage on Senior Bonds, including annual Debt Service on 
the proposed Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds and Future Bonds, in each Fiscal Year of the 
Forecast Period, demonstrates compliance with the Senior Bond Rate Covenant of 125% of 
Senior Aggregate Annual Debt Service.    Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022

PROJECTION OF DEBT SERVICE AND AVAILABILITY PAYMENT COVERAGE  
A projection of coverage was prepared for informational purposes only to demonstrate the 
ability of the Department to meet all of its secured and unsecured obligations, which includes 
(a) all Debt Service on existing Senior and Subordinate Bonds, the proposed Series 2022GHI 
Senior Bonds, and Future Bonds and (b) the annual APM System Capital availability payments 
(APs)13 and the ConRAC Capital APs, both of which are unsecured obligations of the Department 
that are not required to be included in the calculation of debt service coverage under the 
Revenue Bond Indentures.   
The total annual revenues used to calculate coverage for informational purposes only are equal 
to (1) forecast annual Net Pledged Revenues plus (2) forecast annual customer facility charge 
(CFC) revenues that are currently expected by the Department to be used to pay the ConRAC 
Capital APs.   
The projection of coverage (including debt service plus the annual APM Capital AP and ConRAC 
Capital AP) ranges from 157% to 219% between FY 2024 and FY 2030. 
ASSUMPTIONS UNDERLYING THE UPDATED FINANCIAL FORECASTS 
The updated financial forecasts presented in this 2022GHI Letter Report are based on 
information and assumptions provided by, or reviewed with and agreed to by, Department 
management.  The updated forecasts reflect management’s expected course of action during 
the Forecast Period and, in management’s judgment, present fairly the expected financial 
results of the Airport.  Those key factors and assumptions that are significant to the updated 
forecasts are set forth in the Prior Reports and this 2022GH Letter Report.  The Prior Reports 
 
13 See the 2021DE Report for additional information regarding APM and ConRAC Capital APs and the projected 
sources of revenues to make those payments. Ms. Beatrice Hsu 
July 25, 2022 
 A-24 
and this 2022GHI Letter Report should be read in their entirety for an understanding of the 
forecasts and the underlying assumptions. 
In our opinion, the underlying assumptions provide a reasonable basis for the updated 
forecasts.  
However, any forecast is subject to uncertainties.  Inevitably, some assumptions will not be 
realized, and unanticipated events and circumstances may occur.  Therefore, there will be 
differences between the forecast and actual results, and those differences could be material. 
Neither WJ Advisors LLC nor any person acting on our behalf makes any warranty, express or 
implied, with respect to the information, assumptions, forecasts, opinions, or conclusions 
disclosed in this 2022GH Letter Report.  We have no responsibility to update this 2022GHI 
Letter Report for events and circumstances occurring after the date of the 2022GHI Letter 
Report. 
We appreciate the opportunity to serve as the Department’s Airport Consultant in connection 
with this proposed financing.

TOTAL CAPITAL PROGRAM 11,662,366$       74,780$             202,899$           3,929,852$        904,790$           5,575,598$         974,448$             ‐ $                           ‐ $                          11,662,366$       
(a)  Includes projects expected to be completed by FY 2026.  The Department's published existing Capital Program of $15.0 billion includes $3.3 billion of completed projects, developer payments, and remaining Terminal 4 Project costs to be completed after 
      FY 2026 that are not reflected in this Exhibit A.  The Next Airport Capital Program reflected in Exhibit A‐1 includes projects expected to be completed by FY 2029 (including the remaining Terminal 4 Project costs).  The Forecast Period in this 2022GHI Letter 
      Report is through FY 2030, in order to show one full year of financial forecasts following the completion of the Next Airport Capital Program.
(b)  For ConRAC, includes (1) pay‐as‐you‐go CFC revenues and (2) proceeds of special facility obligations expected to be issued by the Department and secured by CFC revenues.  For Enabling Projects, includes $25 million of Dept. of Water & Power funds.
(c)  To be partially funded with the net proceeds of the Series 2022GHI Senior Bonds.   
(d)  The Department expects that additional elements of this project will be constructed as part of the Next Airport Capital Program reflected in Exhibit A‐1 (Next Airport Capital Program), to be completed by the end of FY 2027.    The Department currently 
       expects the additional elements to cost approximately $665 million, as mentioned in footnote (b) on Exhibit A‐1.
(e)  Includes closed circuit television improvements, pipe replacement in the CTA, utilities improvements, bag system improvements, certain future terminal planning costs, and other miscellaneous terminal improvements.       
(f)   Includes improvements to stormwater connections, a replacement of the fire drill training facility, utilities improvements, and other miscellaneous airfield improvements.
(g)   Includes costs to be paid by the Department.  Does not include costs to be paid by the APM Developer during construction.    
(h)  This project is assumed to be an Access Cost Center project.
(i)   Does not include costs to be paid by ConRAC Developer during construction.   
(j)   This project is assumed to be allocated to the Commercial Cost Center.
(k)   Includes drainage and utility improvements.    
(l)   Includes projects for landside accessibility, auxiliary curb project, Bradley West traffic mitigations, and parking structure improvements.
(m)  Includes settlements with local jurisdictions, site preparation, safety, communications, security, and other miscellaneous improvements.
Source:  City of Los Angeles, Department of Airports.

(a)  Source:  City of Los Angeles, Department of Airports.  LAX M&O Expenses + Equipment/Vehicles does not tie exactly to the Department's Adopted Budget due to certain      
      adjustments related to capitalized salaries and benefits and other similar adjustments.  As a result these numbers do not match exactly to Table 12 of the Official Statement.     
(b)  Actual FY 2019, FY 2020, and FY 2021 includes $11.3 million, $33.4 million, and $59.4 million respectively for GASB 68 pension liability amount that is deducted below in the "other adjustments" line, and is only shown through    
       FY 2021 so that Operating Expenses are consistent with Table 12 of the Official Statement.  The GASB 68 pension liability amount or corresponding deduction below is not forecast (not reflected in FY 2022‐FY 2028).    
(c)   Starting in FY 2022, includes costs associated with common use equipment transitioned from an airline consortium to the Deparment.  Starting in FY 2024, includes the estimated APM M&O AP and ConRAC M&O AP.  FY 2025 reflects 
        the first full Fiscal Year for those items.  The ConRAC M&O AP included in this exhibit does not include the variable M&O expenses of the ConRAC to be paid by ConRAC Concessionaires.
(d)   Includes expenses excluded from LAX M&O Expenses, including certain expenses paid with grants/other sources.  Actual FY 2019, FY 2020, and FY 2021 amounts also includes the deduction of $11.3 million, $33.4 million, and 
        $59.4 million of GASB 68 pension liability.  Other adjustments are not forecast as they are not expected to have a material impact on future LAX M&O Expenses.    
(e)  Includes equipment/vehicle expenses under $100K. Equipment/vehicle expenses over $100K paid for by Department funds and amortized over the useful life of the asset.

Forecast
The forecasts presented in this exhibit were prepared using information from the sources identified and assumptions provided by, or reviewed with and agreed to by, Department management, as described in the accompanying text.  
Inevitably, some of the assumptions used to develop the forecasts will not be realized and unanticipated events and circumstances may occur.  Therefore, there are likely to be differences between the forecast and actual results, and 
those differences may be material.
