Los Angeles Fire and Police Pension Plan (“LAFPP”) .  The LAFPP, established in 1899 and 
incorporated into the Charter in 1923, represents contributory plans covering uniformed fire, police, and 
some Department of Harbor and some Department of Airports police.  As of June 30, 2021, the date of its 
most recent actuarial valuation, the LAFPP had 12,823 active members (including 124 in Harbor and 93 in 
Airport), 13,527 retired members and beneficiaries, and 633 vested former members.  
Six tiers of benefits are provided, depending on the date of the member’s hiring.  No active 
members are in Tier 1, while Tier 2 had only 5 active members as of June 30, 2021, although both tiers 
have beneficiaries.  Sixty-four percent of active members are in Tier 5, and 30 percent are in Tier 6. 
Amortization of UAAL may be calculated differently for different tiers.  A Charter amendment 
adopted by City voters on March 8, 2011 provided the LAFPP Board with greater flexibility to establish 
amortization and plan funding policies.  Under the LAFPP Board’s current actuarial funding policy, 
actuarial gains or losses are amortized over 20 years; changes in actuarial assumptions and cost methods 
are amortized over 20 years; plan amendments are amortized over 15 years; and actuarial funding surpluses 
are amortized over 30 years. 
Similar to LACERS, LAFPP has adopted various asset smoothing methods.  Generally, market 
gains or losses are recognized over seven years, so that approximately 1/7 of market losses or gains are 
recognized each year in the actuarial valuation.  LAFPP uses a 40 percent market corridor, so that the 
actuarial value of assets must be between 60 percent and 140 percent of the market value of assets.  If the 
actuarial value falls below 60 percent or rises above 140 percent of market value, the system must recognize 
the excess returns or losses, respectively, in that year without smoothing. 
Within the LAFPP, there is a Deferred Retirement Option Plan (“DROP”).  This voluntary plan 
allows members to retire, for pension purposes only, after they are eligible to retire and have completed at 
least 25 years of service.  A member entering DROP continues to work and receive salary and benefits as 
an active employee but stops accruing additional salary and service credits for retirement purposes.  While 
in DROP, the member’s retirement benefit is deposited into an interest-bearing account that is distributed 
to the member when he or she leaves City service.  Participation in DROP is generally limited to a maximum 
of five years.  The City’s actuary assumes that 95 percent of eligible active members will elect DROP prior 
to retirement.  As of June 30, 2021, 1,484 active members participated in DROP. 
Based on the advice of its actuary, the LAFPP Board reduced its assumed rate of investment return 
from 7.50 percent to 7.25 percent in 2017, lowering it again to 7.00 percent in May 2020 (lowering its 
inflation assumption from 3.00 percent to 2.75 percent as well).  In addition to the economic assumptions, 
the LAFPP Board adopted the actuary’s recommendations to adjust various other assumptions such as 
retirement, termination, and disability incidence rates.  There were no changes in the mortality assumptions 
since the Board adopted new public safety mortality assumptions in December 2019.  Adoption of the 
economic and non-economic assumption changes was estimated to increase City contributions by 2.3 
percent of payroll.  The new assumptions were used in the June 30, 2021 actuarial valuation, which was 
adopted by the Board on November 18, 2021 and determined the City’s contribution rate for Fiscal 
Year 2022-23.

annual payroll. H-10

Historically, plan members did not contribute to offset the City’s costs of retiree healthcare subsidy 
benefits, as all such costs were funded from the employer’s contribution and investment returns thereon.  
In 2011, the City negotiated with the sworn bargaining units the option of a 2 percent active employee 
contribution to offset the cost of retiree healthcare for its sworn workforce hired before July 1, 2011.  Sworn H-12 
employees hired on and after July 1, 2011 are members of Tier 6, which requires an additional 2 percent 
contribution to offset the cost of retiree healthcare.  Employees who contribute to retiree healthcare benefits 
are vested in future subsidy increases authorized by the retirement board. For those sworn employees that 
opted not to make an additional contribution to offset the cost of retiree healthcare, their retiree health 
subsidy has been frozen and cannot surpass the maximum subsidy level in effect as of July 1, 2011.  
A consolidated lawsuit challenged the LAFPP Board’s exercise of its discretion to annually 
increase the subsidy for sworn employees.  On May 2, 2022, the court ruled that LAFPP was not required 
to grant the unions the maximum possible increase in the retiree medical subsidy.  Rather, LAFPP retained 
the discretion on the amount of any increase.  The case will now proceed as to whether the LAFPP abused 
its discretion in the affected years.  See “LITIGATION”.

LITIGATION 
The City is routinely a party to a variety of pending and threatened lawsuits and administrative 
proceedings that may affect the General Fund of the City. The following list of certain newly completed, 
pending or threatened litigation matters involving the City was prepared by the Office of the City Attorney, 
and includes matters with a potential exposure of $10 million or more. For all pending or threatened 
litigation matters and administrative proceedings not listed below, the City believes, based on current facts 
and circumstances, that a final determination of such matters, either individually or in the aggregate, should 
not materially affect the General Fund’s financial position.  Certain litigation or administrative proceedings 
discussed below, if determined in a final and conclusive manner adverse to the City, may, individually or 
in the aggregate, materially affect the General Fund’s financial position.    
THE FOLLOWING LIST HAS BEEN TRUNCATED FROM MATERIALS PROVIDED 
BY THE CITY TO ONLY INCLUDE LITIGATION ADDRESSED IN AND RELATING TO THE 
FOREGOING EXCERPTS. 
*** 
1. Los Angeles Police Protective League and United Firefighters of Los Angeles City v. Board 
of Fire and Police Pension Commissioners v. City of Los Angeles. 
In this case plaintiffs seek a judgment declaring that their letter of agreement with the City requires 
the Board of Fire and Police Pension Commissioners (the "Board") to increase the retirees' medical subsidy 
by the maximum amount allowable per year under the Administrative Code.  The City prevailed on a 
demurrer, but the Court of Appeal reversed and issued a remitter, sending the case back to the trial court to 
resolve disputed factual issues.  A bench trial occurred from September 26 to September 28, 2016.  
Following the bench trial, the court issued a tentative decision in favor of the plaintiffs.  In November 2016, 
the trial court ruled in favor of the plaintiffs' claim with respect to the medical subsidy.  The City appealed 
the trial court ruling.  On October 30, 2018, the appellate court reversed the trial court and ordered that the 
case be remanded for a new trial. 
On August 10, 2017, the Los Angeles Police Protective League filed an additional lawsuit against 
the LAFPP Board and the City in Los Angeles County Superior Court.  The complaint, as supplemented, 
alleges that the Board should have raised the retiree subsidy to the maximum amount of 7 percent for the 
fiscal year beginning July 1, 2017 rather than the 6 percent then awarded and for the fiscal years thereafter.  
This case has been consolidated with the case discussed above.  In October 2021, the court conducted a 
three day trial.  On May 2, 2022, the court ruled that the letter of agreement did not require the City (through 
the LAFPP Board) to grant the unions the maximum possible increase in the retiree medical subsidy.  
Rather, the LAFPP Board retained the discretion on the amount of any increase.  The case will now proceed 
as to whether the LAFPP Board abused its discretion in the years it did not grant the maximum possible 
increase. [PAGE INTENTIONALLY LEFT BLANK] APPENDIX I

Second Party Opinion 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY  
 
ISSUER 
Department of Airports of the City of Los Angeles, California 
OPINION ON 
Los Angeles International Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds 2022 Series G (Private Activity/AMT) (Green 
Bonds) 
Los Angeles International Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds 2022 Series I (Governmental Purpose/Non-AMT) 
(Green Bonds) 
GREEN STANDARD AND CATEGORIES 
 
 Clean Transportation 
 Green Buildings 
KEYWORDS 
Green buildings, LEED, airport, transportation emission reductions, California 
EVALUATION DATE 
July 25, 2022 
SUMMARY 
Kestrel Verifiers is of the opinion that the Los Angeles International Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds 2022 
Series G (Private Activity/AMT) (Green Bonds) (“Series 2022G Bond s”) and the Los Angeles International 
Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds 2022 Series I (Governmental Purpose/Non-AMT) (Green Bonds) (“Series 
2022I Bonds” and collectively, the “Series 2022GI Bonds") are impactful and conform with the four core 
components of the Green Bond Principles 2021 as follows: 
 Use of Proceeds 
The Series 2022GI Bonds will finance a portion of the costs to develop sustainable infrastructure and 
terminal modernization projects at the Los Angeles International Airport  (collectively, the “Projects”). 
Bond-financed building modernizations are all expected to achieve at least LEED Silver certification and 
align with sustainability goals of the Department and the City of Los Angeles  (“Department”). The 
Automated People Mover project is expected to achieve Envision Sustainable Infrastructure certification 
and will alleviate traffic congestion, increase access to public transit, and reduce associated greenhouse 
gas emissions. The Series 2022GI Bonds align with the Green Buildings and Clean Transportation eligible 
categories under the Green Bond Principles. 
 Process for Project Evaluation and Selection 
A documented need for terminal modernizations, improved landside airport access, and a comprehensive 
vision for sustainability have shaped the Projects. The ambitious sustainability targets of the financed 
Projects are aligned with the Department’s formal commitments to sustainability in all aspects of design, 
construction and operation of new capital projects. 
 Management of Proceeds 
Proceeds from the  Series 2022GI Bonds  will be managed from distinct accounts and used to fund the 
Projects, make deposits to certain funds and accounts, and pay costs of issuance. The Series 2022G 
Bonds will be used to acquire portions of the North Terminal Improvement Project and the Terminal 4 
Modernization Program in accordance with airline agreements and to pay for portions of the Terminal 5 
and Tom Bradley International Terminal Vertical Core projects. The Series 2022I Bonds will pay the final 
milestone payment to the developer for the Automated People Mover project based on documentation of 
project completion.  
I-1Kestrel Verifiers | Second Party Opinion Executive Summary ii 
 Reporting 
The Department will post continuing disclosures to the Municipal Securities Rulema king Board (“MSRB”) 
annually through the Electronic Municipal Market Access  (“EMMA”) system. The Department provides 
comprehensive reporting on progress toward sustainability goals in annual Sustainability Reports. In 
connection with the Series 2022GI Bonds, the Department intends to add a Green Bonds section to the  
annual Sustainability Report in 2023. 
 Impact and Alignment with United Nations Sustainable Development Goals 
By financing terminal renovations expected to meet green building standards and the A utomated People 
Mover project expected to achieve Envision Sustainable Infrastructure certification,  the Series 2022GI 
Bonds support and advance multiple UN SDGs, including Goa ls 7: Affordable and Clean Energy  and 
11: Sustainable Cities and Communities.

Kestrel Verifiers | Second Party Opinion  1

GREEN BONDS DESIGNATION 
Kestrel Verifiers, an Approved Verifier accredited by the Climate Bonds Initiative, conducted an independent 
external review of the Los Angeles International Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds 2022 Series G (Private 
Activity/AMT) (Green Bonds) (“Series 2022G Bonds”) and the Los Angeles International Airport, Senior 
Revenue Bonds 2022 Series I (Governmental Purpose/Non-AMT) (Green Bonds) (“Series 2022I Bonds” and 
collectively, the “Series 2022GI Bonds") to evaluate conformance with the Green Bond Principles (June 2021) 
established by the International Capital Market Association. Our team for this engagement included analysts 
with backgrounds in environmental science and social science. 
This Second Party Opinion reflects our review of the uses and allocation of proceeds , oversight and 
conformance of the Series 2022GI Bonds with the Green Bond Principles. In our opinion, the Series 2022GI 
Bonds are impactful, aligned with the four core components of the Green Bond Principles, and qualify for 
Green Bonds designation. 
ABOUT THE ISSUER 
The Department of Airports of the City of Los Angeles (“Department”) is a self- supporting department of 
the City of Los Angeles (the “City”) that manages two airports: Los Angeles International Airport and Van 
Nuys Airport. Both airports contribute significantly to the regional economy, collectively providing more than 
3,000 jobs in 2021. The Los Angeles Economic Development Corporation estimated that each international 
flight landing at Los Angeles International Airport was worth up to $623 million in economic activity resulting 
from passengers, cargo, employment and operational activities.1 In 2019, Los Angeles International Airport 
served more than 88 million passengers.2  
 
1 “Airport Basics,” Los Angeles World Airports, accessed January 21, 2022, https://www.lawa.org/lawa-our-lax/airport-basics.  
2 “10-Year Summary of Passengers,” Los Angeles World Airports, accessed January 7, 2022, https://www.lawa.org/lawa- investor-
relations/statistics-for-lax/10-year-summary/passengers.  
I-3 
Kestrel Verifiers | Second Party Opinion  2 
In 2021, the Department received recognition for its sustainability practices, including being the Aviation 
Winner at California Transportation Foundation’s Transportation Awards. The Los Angeles International 
Airport received a Civic Award from the US Green Building Council of Los Angeles, which recognizes 
exceptional achievements in sustainable building. Los Angeles International Airport also received an Honor 
Award in the 2022 Engineering Excellence Awards competition by the American Council of Engineering 
Companies California.
3 
The Department has robust environmental commitments which are addressed through energy management, 
reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, water and waste management, noise management, land use 
planning, and regulatory compliance. The 2019 Sustainability Action Plan
4 highlights the Department’s 
history as a leader in airport sustainability and outlines plans to reach zero carbon emissions by 2045 and 
reduce environmental impacts. The Department has updated its Sustainability Action Plan to align with the 
Los Angeles mayor’s updated Sustainable City pLAn.
5 Major capital projects have been strategically selected 
and designed to minimize energy use and reduce the Department’s carbon footprint. In 2019, Van Nuys 
Airport built the first of its solar projects and became the first general aviation airport to offe r sustainable 
alternative jet fuel. Other notable programs and policies of the Department include:  
 Sustainable Design and Construction Requirements – All new buildings and projects at the airports 
must be built to at least LEED Silver standards or the Los Angeles City Green Building Code.  
 Alternative Fuel Program at Los Angeles International Airport – All airport lessees and operators are 
required to drive vehicles that are not powered by petroleum -derived gasoline or diesel fuel. As of 
2019, 100% of airport operators drive compliant vehicles.  
 Electric Vehicle Purchasing Policy – Adopted in 2017, the Electric Vehicle Purchasing Policy aims to 
improve air quality in and around the airports as the Department increases electric vehicle purchases 
to 100% by 2035.   
 Single-use Plastic Water Bottle Phase -out Policy – Beginning in June 2023, the Department will no 
longer allow sale or distribution of single-use plastic water bottles at airports.   
 Food Donation Program at Los Angeles International Airport – All businesses that provide or sell 
food are required to participate in a food donation program.   
 Los Angeles International Airport Dunes Restoration – The Department began the Los Angeles 
International Airport Sand Dunes Restoration Project in 1986 by dedicating 307 acres on the western 
portion of the airport as a natural wildlife preserve. Since then, the Department has planted native 
species, eliminated invasive plants, and removed approximately 32,000 square feet of hardscape 
including old streets and sidewalks.   
 El Segundo Blue Butterfly Habitat Management – In 1976, the US Department of Fish and Wildlife 
placed the El Segundo Blue Butterfly on the endangered species list. This butterfly was later found 
in the Los Angeles International Airport Sand Dunes H abitat. As of 2020, populations of the 
endangered butterfly within the habitat restoration area had grown to 5,300-6,500.   
 Ground Support Equipment Emissions Reduction Policy – This program, developed in 2015, reduces 
emissions from aircraft support equipment such as tractors, tugs, tows, catering trucks, belt loaders, 
and fuel trucks. One year after implementation of the policy, 40% of the Los Angeles International 
Airport’s ground support vehicles were fully electric. The transition to an electrified fleet has led to 
reduced emissions and the Department exceeded its goal five years ahead of the deadline.  
 Airport Carbon Accreditation – The Los Angeles International Airport is one of only 17 airports in the 
US to receive the Level 3 ‘Optimization’ accreditation from Airport Carbon Accreditation, the only 
institutionally endorsed, airport-specific, global carbon management certification pr ogram. Airport 
Carbon Accreditation provides airports with a  common framework for active carbon management 
with measurable goals.  
 
3 “2021 Sustainability Report,” Los Angeles World Airports, 2021, https://cloud1lawa.box.com/s/pq459i5b4qt4rtw1r2i3296tkixaigxj.  
4 “Boldly Moving to Zero: Sustainability Action Plan,” Los Angeles World Airports, 2019, https://www.lawa.org/lawa-sustainability.  
5 “Sustainable City pLAn,” City of Los Angeles, 2019, https://plan.lamayor.org/.

Kestrel Verifiers | Second Party Opinion  3 
ALIGNMENT TO GREEN STANDARDS6 
Use of Proceeds 
The proceeds of the Series 2022GI Bonds will be used to pay or reimburse 
the Department for a portion of the costs of sustainable infrastructure and 
terminal modernizations at the Los Angeles International Airport  
(collectively, the “Projects”). All bond-financed building modernizations are 
expected to achieve at least LEED Silver certification and align with 
sustainability goals of the Department and the City of Los Angeles. The 
Automated People Mover (“APM”) project will alleviate traffic congestion, 
increase access to public transit, and reduce  associated greenhouse gas 
emissions. The Series 2022GI Bonds align with the Green Buildings and 
Clean Transportation eligible categories under the Green Bond Principles. 
North Terminal Improvement Pro ject: Proceeds from  the Series  2022 G Bonds  will partially finance 
replacement of Terminal 3 in partnership with Delta Air Lines. Construction of Terminal 3 will achieve LEED 
Silver certification at a minimum, and will include energy saving technologies in escalators, high efficiency 
LED lighting and sensors to reduce energy use for lighting. Low-flow water fixtures will reduce water use by 
45% relative to the baseline as per LEED criteria, and 75% of construction and demolition waste  will be 
diverted from landfills. Terminal 3 is expected to achieve at least a 26% improvement in energy efficiency 
due to a high -performance building envelope. 7 The overall project includes construction of a walkway 
between Terminal 3 and the Tom Bradley International Terminal, areas to access the Automated People 
Mover, addition of new gates, expansion of passenger seating areas, upgrades to restrooms, and space for 
concessions. Construction is expected to be completed in Q3 2023. 
Terminal 4 Modernization Program: Proceeds from the Series 2022G Bonds will also finance improvements 
to Terminal 4, where American Airlines currently operates. The project is designed to achieve a LEED Silver 
certification and includes consolidation of 30 gates to i mprove passenger movement; a new connection 
between Terminals 4 and 5 ; and offices for airlines, the Transportation Security Administration, and law 
enforcement agencies. The project also includes central areas for  ticketing, security and baggage claim , 
and a connection to the Automated People Mover. Approximately 75% of construction and demolition waste 
will be diverted from landfills. The project is anticipated to reduce indoor water use by approximately 20% 
relative to the baseline LEED criteria . The Department will purchase renewable energy credits for at least 
20% of energy used in the building. Terminal 4 improvements are expected to be completed by 2026. 
Terminal 5 and Tom Bradley International Terminal Vertical Core projects: The additions and modernizations 
designed to connect the terminals with the Automated People Mover will achieve LEED Silver certification 
at a minimum. In order to achieve green building certifications, the projects include energy efficient designs, 
sustainable building materials, building level energy metering and low-flow water fixtures.  
Impact of Financed Activities 
The Projects are aligned with the Department’s goal to achieve carbon neutrality by 2045 and will meet the 
Department’s Sustainable Design and Construction Requirements. The modernizations support the transition 
to carbon neutrality in the building sector by incorporating water fixtures to reduce water use, locally sourced 
building materials, responsible waste management, and  energy-efficient design features. Env ironmental 
benefits include reduced greenhouse gas emissions in line with targets, diverting waste from landfills, and 
water conservation.  
Direct emissions from fossil fuel combustion associated with heating, cooling and operating residential and 
commercial buildings account for approximately 29% of total US greenhouse gas emissions, and 
 
6 Green Bonds are any type of bond instrument where the proceeds will be exclusively applied to finance or refinance eligible Green 
Projects which are aligned with the four core components of ICMA’s Green Bond Principles. 
7 Compared to ASHRAE 90.1-2007; Factors such as insulation, Solar Heat Gain Coefficients, and roof design to minimize the heat island 
effect contribute to the energy use reduction. 
Green Standard 
 
 
Eligible Project Categories: 
 Green Buildings 
 Clean Transportation 
I-5 
Kestrel Verifiers | Second Party Opinion  4 
approximately 25% of statewide greenhouse gas emissions in California.8 The building modernizations align 
with statewide targets to reduce building-related emissions by at least 40% below 1990 levels by 2030.9 
While aviation is a significant source of global greenhouse gas emissions, Kestrel views constructi on of 
airport facilities that meet green building standards as eligible for financing with Green Bond proceeds. In 
Kestrel’s opinion, the mechanisms for reducing the carbon footprint of airline travel may be viewed distinctly 
from the emissions associated with constructing and operating the supporting facilities. 
Automated People Mover (“APM”): Proceeds from the Series 2022I Bonds will finance a portion of a grade-
separated and fully electrified train  system which connect s the regional public transit system , the 
consolidated rental car facility (ConRAC)  and the Central Terminal Area . The Project is one of five major 
projects in the Department’s Landside Access Modernization Program, and is designed to resolve significant 
congestion and unpredictable travel times caused by the lack of direct connection with larger public 
transportation networks. 
The scope of the APM project includes the rail line, six stations, train cars, and a train maintenance facility 
which is expected to achieve LEED  Gold certification. Proceeds of the Series 2022I  Bonds will finance the 
final milestone payment for the project. The 2.25-mile elevated guideway was completed in April 2022 and 
the entire system is expected to be completed by Q1 2024. The APM, which will eliminate roughly 3,200 
daily shuttle trips  and improve access to the regional public transit  system, will reduce transportation-
related emissions by alleviating traffic congestion and making it easier for employees and travelers alike to 
use public transportation. The APM aligns with the Clean Transportation project category of the Green Bond 
Principles. 
The Department is expected to pursue Envision certification for the Automated People Mover Project . 
Envision is a holistic sustainability framework and rating system for physical infrastructure developed by 
the Harvard University Graduate School of Design and the Institute for Sustainable Infrastructure. The 
Institute was founded by the  American Public Works Association, the American Council of Engineering 
Companies, and the American Society of Civil Engineers.10 The Envision framework is widely accepted and 
promoted by the American Society of Civil Engineers as a best practice for sustainable design. Just as LEED 
is a recognizable standar d for green building design, Envision is a recognizable standard for sustainable 
infrastructure of all types. The Envision framework includes 64 sustainability and resilience indicators 
(credits) organized around five categories: Quality of Life, Leadership, Resource Allocation, Natural World, 
and Climate and Resilience . The framework seeks to collectively address components of community 
development, human health and well- being, mobility and emissions, wildlife conservation, life cycles of 
materials, and mo re. The APM project is anticipated to pursue, at a minimum, the Envision Verification 
level and may achieve Envision Platinum, the highest potential level of achievement.

8 “Building Decarbonization ,” California Air Resources Board, accessed July 14, 2022, https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-

Climate Risk and Transition Alignment11 
Mitigation of transition risk requires planning for the necessary structural changes to address climate change 
and the transition to a low -carbon economy, with recognition of the risks associated with inaction.  The 
Projects address climate transition risks by incorporating energy efficiency and green building features to 
directly reduce greenhouse gas emissions and advance decarbonization goals. The reduced congestion and 
improved access to public transit as a result of the Automated People Mover  mitigates climate transition 
risks in the transportation sector as well as regional systemic risks associated with unabated greenhouse 
gas emissions from vehicles. 
The Series 2022GI Bonds also finance activities which align with the  just transition, characterized by the 
equitable inclusion and accommodation of all individuals, with a special focus on disadvantaged groups who 
may be directly or indirectly affected by the structural changes necessary for the transition to a low-carbon 
economy. The Los Angeles International Airport is located west of communities considered to be especially 
vulnerable to pollution based on environmental, health and socioeconomic data.
12 By reducing transportation 
emissions at the Airport  through the APM project, the Department is reducing negative health impacts 
associated with air pollution on vulnerable communities in the area. These are primarily Latinx 
neighborhoods in Southeast Los Angeles. Compared to predominantly white neighborhoods, communities 
of color in the US have historically faced higher levels of overall pollution and experienced poorer health 
outcomes as a consequence. 13 While the  APM project does not directly confront environmental justice 
concerns, reduced  greenhouse gas  emissions in the area will effectively improve air quality for the 
surrounding vulnerable communities, thus facilitating the just transition. 
Process for Project Evaluation and Selection 
The Department’s clear commitment to reducing environmental impacts led to  incorporation of green 
building designs. An identified need to reduce congestion and improve transit connectivity  led the 
Department to prioritize the Automated People Mover project. The ambitious sustainability targets for these 
projects are mainly driven by the City of Los Angeles’ climate action goals and the Department’s strong 
commitment to sustainability in operations and capital projects.  
Further, the Design Guidelines for Los Angeles International Airport require life cycle assessments for 
buildings to minimize environmental impact, as well as steps to improve energy efficiency, increase use of 
renewables, conserve water, increase use of recycled materials, and improve health and comfort for building 
occupants. 
The Projects are impactful and directly advance targets to reach carbon neutrality by 2045, adopted in the 
Department’s 2019 Sustainability Action Plan and the City of Los Angeles Sustainable City pLAn (2019).  
Management of Proceeds 
Proceeds from the Series 2022GI Bond s will be managed from distinct accounts and used to fund the 
Projects, make deposits to certain funds and accounts, and to pay costs of issuance. The Series 2022G 
Bonds will be us ed to acquire portions of the North Terminal Improvement Project and the Terminal 4 
Modernization Program in accordance with airline agreements and to pay for portions of the Terminal 5 and 
Tom Bradley International Terminal Vertical Core projects . The Series 2022I Bonds will pay the final 
milestone payment to the developer for the APM  project based on documentation of project completion. 
Prior to allocation, proceeds may be held temporarily in Permitted Investments, as defined by the Indenture. 
Permitted Investments include, but are not limited to, government obligations, highly rated obligations of 
any state, money market funds and prime commercial paper. 
 
11 Climate change poses significant systemic risks to US financial systems and municipal issuers. These risks may broadly be div ided 
into physical risk, transition risk and societal risk. Physical risk includes effects of climate change on physical assets, such as extreme 
weather events and sea level rise. Transition risk includes market and technology risks, reputational risks, policy risks and legal risks. 
Societal risk includes risk to stable democracies, risk to civil liberties and human rights, risk to labor supply, and risk to public health. 
Mitigation of transition risk requires planning for the necessary structural changes to address climate change and societal inequity with 
recognition of the risks associated with inaction. We refer to this as the just transition to a decarbonized economy, or the just transition. 
12 “CalEnviroScreen,” California Office of Environmental Health Hazard Assessment and California Environmental Protection Agency , 
accessed July 14, 2022, https://oehha.ca.gov/calenviroscreen/report/calenviroscreen-30. 
13 “The Most Pressing Environmental Health Conditions for Latinx Communities in Southeast L.A.,” Neighborhood Data for Social Change, 
KCET, September 9, 2021, https://www.kcet.org/neighborhood-data-for-social-change/the-most-pressing-environmental-health-
conditions-for-latinx-communities-in-southeast-l-a.

Kestrel Verifiers | Second Party Opinion  6 
Reporting 
The Department will submit continuing disclosure reports to the Municipal Securities Rulemaking 
Board (“MSRB”) as long as the Series 2022GI Bonds are outstanding. The Department will also provide 
reports in the event of material developments. This reporting will be done annually and at other required 
times on the Electronic Municipal Market Access (“EMMA”) system operated by the MSRB.  
The Department provides annual comprehensive reporting on progress toward sustainability goals in 
Sustainability Reports available at lawa.org/lawa-sustainability. Reported metrics include greenhouse gas 
emissions (MTCO
2e), tons of materials recycled, square footage of construction built to green building 
standards, fuel usage and energy usage. The Department also maintains a dashboard with greenhouse gas 
emission accounting available at: lawa.org/lawa-sustainability/sustainability-elements-air. 
The Department intends to add a section to the annual Sustainability Report for updates on these bond-
financed Projects. The Department expects to provide annual updates for investors in the Sustainability 
Report until all LEED certifications are received and projects are complete. Updates are expected to include: 
the status of construction, energy performance and other project-related metrics such as percent of waste 
diverted from the landfill and confirmation of LEED certifications.  
IMPACT AND ALIGNMENT WITH UN SDGS 
The Department’s Projects are helping to address UN SDGs 7 and 11. By financing several airport terminals 
and other energy efficient facilities that are expected to achieve at least LEED Silver certification, the Series 
2022GI Bonds address Target 7.3. Construction of the Automated People Mover will reduce transportation-
related greenhouse gas emissions and alleviate traffic congestion in and around Los Angeles International 
Airport, thereby advancing Targets 11.2 and 11.6.  
Full text of the Targets for Goals 7 and 11 is available in Appendix A, with additional information available 
on the United Nations website:  un.org/sustainabledevelopment 
 
 
Affordable and Clean Energy (Target 7.3) 
Possible Indicators 
 Reduced greenhouse gas emissions as a result of energy efficiency features 
  
 
Sustainable Cities and Communities (Targets 11.2, 11.6) 
Possible Indicators 
 Avoided greenhouse gas emissions (tons CO2eq) 
 Avoided air pollutant emissions (other than greenhouse gases) 
CONCLUSION 
Based on our independent external review, the  Los Angeles International Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds 
2022 Series G (Private Activity/AMT) (Green Bonds) and the Los Angeles International Airport, Senior 
Revenue Bonds 2022 Series I (Governmental Purpose/Non-AMT) (Green Bonds)  conform, in al l material 
respects, with the Green Bond Principles  (2021) and are in complete alignment with two  eligible project 
categories: Clean Transportation and Green Buildings. We find the Series 2022GI Bonds to be impactful as 
the financed activities directly advance climate action goals shared by the Department and the City of Los 
Angeles by financing building renovations expected to achieve  robust green building certifications and 
infrastructure to reduce traffic congestion and improve access to public transportation.

UN SDG TARGET DEFINITIONS  
 
Target 7.3 
By 2030, double the global rate of improvement in energy efficiency 
Target 11.2 
By 2030, provide access to safe, affordable, accessible and sustainable transport systems for all, improving 
road safety, notably by expanding public transport, with special attention to the needs of those in vulnerable 
situations, women, children, persons with disabilities and older persons 
Target 11.6 
By 2030, reduce the adverse per capita environmental impact of cities, including by paying special attention 
to air quality and municipal and other waste management
